papers.title: Statistical Arbitrage Pairs Trading Strategies: Review and Outlook
papers.summary: This survey reviews multiple approaches to pairs trading, a mean-reversion strategy exploiting temporary divergences between historically related securities. It highlights distance, cointegration, time-series, and stochastic control methods, evaluating their profitability, robustness, and limitations under market frictions. The paper synthesizes over 100 studies to assess when and why pairs trading works.
key_findings: Pairs trading generates historically significant excess returns, but profitability declines when accounting for transaction costs and non-converging pairs.
Cointegration-based selection improves reliability of mean-reversion compared to simple distance or correlation metrics.
Profitability is linked to temporary mispricings driven by delayed information diffusion and market frictions.
works_best_when: assets exhibit stable long-term relationships or cointegration
markets have slower information diffusion or temporary inefficiencies
fails_when: pairs do not converge or relationships are spurious
transaction costs and market frictions outweigh spread profits
implementation_notes: Maps directly to mean-reversion strategies using spread divergence and convergence signals
Cointegration testing can improve pair selection over simple distance-based methods
Threshold-based entry/exit rules (e.g., standard deviation bands) are commonly used for signal generation
papers.citation: Krauss (2016) Statistical Arbitrage Pairs Trading Strategies: Review and Outlook

papers.summary: Replace this with a short grounded summary of the paper's main claim about trend-following strategies.
key_findings: Trend-following signals tend to work better in persistent directional regimes.
Longer windows usually trade off responsiveness for stability.

ema_fast.note: Shorter lookbacks respond faster but may be noisier.

ema_slow.note: Longer slow windows reduce noise but may lag.

adx_threshold.note: Higher thresholds require stronger trend confirmation.

implementation_notes: Maps naturally to trend_follower and MACD family candidates.

papers.title: Template Entry: RSI and Bollinger Mean Reversion
papers.summary: Replace this with a short grounded summary of the paper's main claim about mean-reversion behavior.
key_findings: Mean-reversion setups tend to perform better in ranging conditions.
Extreme signals may fail in strong trends.
Volatility bands can help frame reversal opportunities.

bb_window.note: Shorter windows react faster to recent volatility.

bb_std.note: Wider bands may reduce false entries in volatile markets.

rsi_window.note: Shorter RSI windows are more reactive.

rsi_entry.note: Lower thresholds require deeper oversold conditions.

rsi_exit.note: Higher exits hold longer but may give back more gains.

works_best_when: prices oscillate around a local mean
implementation_notes: Maps directly to the current mean_reversion strategy family.

papers.title: Template Entry: Volume Filter for Signal Confirmation
papers.summary: Replace this with a short grounded summary of how volume may improve signal quality.
key_findings: Volume can help confirm whether a move has broader participation.
Low-volume reversals may be less reliable.
Volume filters can reduce trade frequency while improving selectivity.

volume_ma_window.note: Used to compare current volume against a recent baseline.

fails_when: volume data is noisy or unreliable
volume is not informative for the asset
implementation_notes: Useful for future experimental strategies such as RSI plus volume filter or breakout plus volume confirmation.
